message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 13
Recent Green phase: Restructured quotePremium to additive form: policyBase = itemBaseTotal(items); surcharges = sum of itemSurcharge(item) where curse adds CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE(0.5)*itemOwnBase(item); initialAssessment = INITIAL_ASSESSMENT_RATE(0.1)*policyBase; premium = ceil(policyBase + surcharges + initialAssessment + PROCESSING_FEE). Added helpers itemOwnBase(item) (COMPONENT_PRICE for components, BASE_PREMIUM[type] otherwise) and itemSurcharge(item).

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run tests with `pnpm test`. Evaluate naming first, then Four Rules of Simple Design, measure APP mass / cognitive / McCabe before and after. Note: there may now be conceptual overlap between itemOwnBase (per-item base) and itemBaseTotal/componentBase (grouped base) — assess whether consolidation improves the metrics or whether they serve genuinely different purposes (per-item surcharge base vs grouped block pricing). Keep changes minimal and metric-driven.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Tests green (13 passing). ESLint reports zero smells. Now let me complete the PRE measurements.

**Step 0 — ESLint PRE**: 0 smells, 0 cognitive-complexity warnings across the file. Surface is clean.

**Step 1 — Naming Evaluation**:

- `itemOwnBase(item)` — returns the per-item base price used as the curse-surcharge base. Name reveals "this item's own base premium." Fits.
- `itemSurcharge(item)` — per-item surcharge (curse). Fits.
- `componentBase(count)` — block pricing for a count of one component type. Name is slightly ambiguous (component vs block), but acceptable.
- `countByType` / `itemBaseTotal` / `quotePremium` — all reveal intent. Fits.

No rename has clear net benefit.

**Step 2 — APP Mass (PRE)** for the two functions flagged for possible overlap:

`itemOwnBase`: ternary(4) + invocation `has`(2) + bindings `item`,`COMPONENT_PRICE`,`BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` (~3) + constant — mass ≈ **11**

`itemBaseTotal`: assignment `total=0`(6) + loop(5) + invocation `countByType`(2) + ternary(4) + invocation `has`(2) + assignment `total+=`(6) + bindings/invocations (~6) — mass ≈ **31**

**Step 3 — McCabe (PRE)**:
- `itemOwnBase`: 1 + ternary = **2**
- `itemSurcharge`: 1 + ternary = **2**
- `componentBase`: 1 + ternary = **2**
- `countByType`: 1 + for + `??` = **3**
- `itemBaseTotal`: 1 + for + ternary = **3**
- `quotePremium`: **1**

The worst McCabe is tied at 3; nothing is an outlier. All functions are at or near floor.

**Assessing the flagged overlap** (`itemOwnBase` vs `itemBaseTotal`/`componentBase`): These serve genuinely different purposes:
- `itemOwnBase` = per-item unit base (curse surcharge base), needs the *individual* item's price ignoring block discounts.
- `itemBaseTotal`/`componentBase` = grouped block pricing where 3-of-a-kind components collapse to a block price.

Consolidating them would conflate "individual item value" with "discounted grouped block value" — the curse surcharge must be 50% of the item's *own* base, NOT a block-discounted share. Merging would either break correctness or require passing flags, raising mass and McCabe. They must stay separate.

**One small DRY/Rule-3 opportunity I can verify with metrics**: `itemOwnBase` and `itemBaseTotal` both contain the branch `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) ? <component price> : BASE_PREMIUM[type]`. But the component-price arm differs (single vs block), so the duplication is only the *classification* test `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(...)`, not the pricing. Extracting that one-call predicate would add an invocation (mass +2 per call site) and a function while removing nothing branch-wise — McCabe unchanged, mass up. Net negative.

Let me confirm there's no accidental duplication worth extracting by checking the `BASE_PREMIUM[type] * count` vs `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type]` relationship — these are structurally the unit price times count, which is genuine, not extractable without cost.

**Decision: no refactoring applied — all four metrics are at or near their floor, naming reveals intent, and the only candidate consolidation (itemOwnBase ↔ itemBaseTotal) would conflate two distinct pricing concepts and worsen the metrics.**

Let me document the per-item-base duplication assessment concretely and confirm tests remain green (unchanged).

content.text:
Refactoring evaluation complete. No code change applied — the implementation is already at the metric floor for the behaviour it expresses.

## Refactoring Report

**PRE measurements** (`/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_02-27-51_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`):

| Function | ESLint smells | McCabe | APP mass | Naming |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `itemOwnBase` | 0 | 2 | ~11 | reveals intent |
| `itemSurcharge` | 0 | 2 | ~6 | reveals intent |
| `componentBase` | 0 | 2 | ~9 | reveals intent |
| `countByType` | 0 | 3 | ~17 | reveals intent |
| `itemBaseTotal` | 0 | 3 | ~31 | reveals intent |
| `quotePremium` | 0 | 1 | ~24 | reveals intent |

- **ESLint smells**: 0 total, 0 cognitive-complexity warnings.
- **Worst McCabe**: 3 (tied, `countByType` / `itemBaseTotal`) — no outlier to target.

**Naming**: All names reveal current intent. No rename has net benefit.

**Flagged overlap assessment (`itemOwnBase` vs `itemBaseTotal`/`componentBase`)**: They serve genuinely different pricing concepts and must stay separate:
- `itemOwnBase` = the individual item's unit base, used as the curse-surcharge base. The curse surcharge must be 50% of the item's *own* undiscounted base.
- `itemBaseTotal` + `componentBase` = grouped block pricing where exactly 3 matching components collapse to a block discount.

Consolidating would conflate per-item value with block-discounted value, risking correctness or forcing flag parameters that raise both McCabe and APP mass. The only literal duplication is the classification test `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(...)`; extracting it as a predicate would add an invocation (mass +2 per call site, +1 function element under Rule 4) while removing zero branches — McCabe unchanged, mass up. Net negative.

**Refactoring applied**: None.

**Reasoning**: Every metric is at or near its floor for the behaviour expressed (most functions McCabe 2–3, all with a single decision point; no ESLint smells; no cognitive warnings). The one candidate consolidation worsens the metrics and clarity. Per Rule 2 (reveals intent) over Rule 3 (DRY) and Rule 4 (fewest elements), keeping the two pricing concepts distinct is correct.

**POST = PRE** (no change). Tests: 13/13 passing. ESLint: 0 smells.

```
🔄 Refactoring Complete:

Smell delta: 0 → 0
Cognitive delta: 0 → 0 (no warnings either side)
APP mass delta: unchanged (no change applied)
McCabe delta: unchanged (worst 3 → 3)
Tests: All passing ✅ (13 passed, 29 todo)
```
